tweets.title: There are no saints here : not even @ShivAroor bhai. 😂 and I understand his pressure. You just cannot ask them difficult questions else they won’t come (I tried it and I know - some of them in-fact asked questions in advance) I’d genuinely love to ask: what aircraft manufacturer anywhere in the world would happily accept an order split across three different production standards? 16 IOC fighters 16 FOC fighters 8 trainers Most OEMs would probably point to the exit. 😂and say that rectangular thing is door. That said, HAL isn’t beyond criticism either. Look at the timelines: 31 Mar 2006: Order for 16 IOC fighters + 4 trainers 23 Dec 2010: Order for 16 FOC fighters + 4 trainers 30 Sep 2014: First production IOC aircraft (SP-1) flies : 8 years after the first order 17 Mar 2020: First production FOC aircraft (SP-21) flies : nearly 10 years after the FOC order Then, after all that, the 83 Tejas Mk1A order received CCS approval only on 13 Jan 2021. The Tejas story has many villains. Procurement decisions, changing configurations, certification timelines, production bottlenecks, and industrial capacity all contributed. Blaming only the IAF or only HAL misses the bigger picture.
tweets.content:
There are no saints here : not even @ShivAroor bhai. 😂 and I understand his pressure. You just cannot ask them difficult questions else they won’t come (I tried it and I know - some of them in-fact asked questions in advance)

I’d genuinely love to ask: what aircraft manufacturer anywhere in the world would happily accept an order split across three different production standards?

16 IOC fighters
16 FOC fighters
8 trainers

Most OEMs would probably point to the exit. 😂and say that rectangular thing is door.

That said, HAL isn’t beyond criticism either. Look at the timelines:

31 Mar 2006: Order for 16 IOC fighters + 4 trainers
23 Dec 2010: Order for 16 FOC fighters + 4 trainers
30 Sep 2014: First production IOC aircraft (SP-1) flies : 8 years after the first order
17 Mar 2020: First production FOC aircraft (SP-21) flies : nearly 10 years after the FOC order

Then, after all that, the 83 Tejas Mk1A order received CCS approval only on 13 Jan 2021.

The Tejas story has many villains. Procurement decisions, changing configurations, certification timelines, production bottlenecks, and industrial capacity all contributed. Blaming only the IAF or only HAL misses the bigger picture.

ghatak (@Neetivaan)

tweets.content:
BREAKING: DAC Clears ₹52,000 Crore Defence Modernisation Package

The Defence Acquisition Council (DAC), chaired by Defence Minister Rajnath Singh, has approved capital acquisition proposals worth ₹52,000 crore to strengthen the combat capabilities of India's Armed Forces.

Indian Army
• AKASH TARANG anti-UAV electronic warfare system
• MPATGM anti-tank missiles
• MRSAM air defence system
• V-SHORADS air defence missiles
• Active Protection System for tanks
• Jet-based kamikaze drone system

Indian Navy
• Multi Influence Ground Mines (MIGM)
• Naval Shipborne Unmanned Aerial System (NSUAS)
• Land-Based Testing Facility for electric propulsion systems

Indian Air Force
• Fixed-Wing High Altitude Pseudo Satellite (FW-HAPS) for persistent ISR, telecommunications and remote sensing.

The approvals are aimed at enhancing India's air defence, anti-drone capabilities, armoured survivability, naval warfare, and long-endurance surveillance capabilities.

Netram Defence Review (@NetramDefence)

₹5,000 Crore Defence Procurement Set for DAC Approval

Defence Acquisition Council (DAC) is expected to clear major procurement proposals worth nearly ₹5,000 crore, aimed at boosting the armed forces' combat capabilities and indigenous defence production.

Key proposals include:

🔹 600 HAMMER precision-guided munitions for the IAF's Rafale, Tejas, and Navy's Rafale Marine fighters
🔹 100 MP-ATGM launchers, 2,300 indigenous anti-tank missiles, and 5 simulators for the Indian Army
🔹 Verba V-SHORADS for Army Air Defence
🔹 High-altitude pseudo satellites, kamikaze drones, drone detection systems, software-defined radios, and Scorpene submarine upgrades

The meeting marks the first DAC session with the new military leadership and is expected to further accelerate India's defence modernisation and Make in India initiatives.

tweets.title: LeT Reshuffles Leadership After Operation Sindoor Pakistan-based terror outfit Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) has reportedly carried out a major leadership reshuffle involving over 15 commanders across Pakistan's Punjab province following Operation Sindoor. According to open-source intelligence reports, the changes, ordered by Hafiz Saeed on 28 June, include the appointment of senior commanders to oversee training centres, recruitment networks, and infiltration routes. Key developments: • Haris Dar has been appointed to head Central Punjab, overseeing major LeT training facilities and infiltration corridors. • Imran Liaqat Bhatti, a senior arms trainer, has been moved to Kasur, which is reportedly emerging as a key training hub after the destruction of LeT's Muridke headquarters during Operation Sindoor. The reshuffle suggests LeT is attempting to reorganise and decentralise its infrastructure, dispersing training activities across multiple locations while reinforcing its command structure after recent operational setbacks.
tweets.content:
LeT Reshuffles Leadership After Operation Sindoor

Pakistan-based terror outfit Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) has reportedly carried out a major leadership reshuffle involving over 15 commanders across Pakistan's Punjab province following Operation Sindoor.

According to open-source intelligence reports, the changes, ordered by Hafiz Saeed on 28 June, include the appointment of senior commanders to oversee training centres, recruitment networks, and infiltration routes.

Key developments:
• Haris Dar has been appointed to head Central Punjab, overseeing major LeT training facilities and infiltration corridors.
• Imran Liaqat Bhatti, a senior arms trainer, has been moved to Kasur, which is reportedly emerging as a key training hub after the destruction of LeT's Muridke headquarters during Operation Sindoor.

The reshuffle suggests LeT is attempting to reorganise and decentralise its infrastructure, dispersing training activities across multiple locations while reinforcing its command structure after recent operational setbacks.

tweets.title: ₹5,000 Crore Defence Procurement Set for DAC Approval Defence Acquisition Council (DAC) is expected to clear major procurement proposals worth nearly ₹5,000 crore, aimed at boosting the armed forces' combat capabilities and indigenous defence production. Key proposals include: 🔹 600 HAMMER precision-guided munitions for the IAF's Rafale, Tejas, and Navy's Rafale Marine fighters 🔹 100 MP-ATGM launchers, 2,300 indigenous anti-tank missiles, and 5 simulators for the Indian Army 🔹 Verba V-SHORADS for Army Air Defence 🔹 High-altitude pseudo satellites, kamikaze drones, drone detection systems, software-defined radios, and Scorpene submarine upgrades The meeting marks the first DAC session with the new military leadership and is expected to further accelerate India's defence modernisation and Make in India initiatives.
tweets.content:
₹5,000 Crore Defence Procurement Set for DAC Approval

Defence Acquisition Council (DAC) is expected to clear major procurement proposals worth nearly ₹5,000 crore, aimed at boosting the armed forces' combat capabilities and indigenous defence production.

Key proposals include:

🔹 600 HAMMER precision-guided munitions for the IAF's Rafale, Tejas, and Navy's Rafale Marine fighters
🔹 100 MP-ATGM launchers, 2,300 indigenous anti-tank missiles, and 5 simulators for the Indian Army
🔹 Verba V-SHORADS for Army Air Defence
🔹 High-altitude pseudo satellites, kamikaze drones, drone detection systems, software-defined radios, and Scorpene submarine upgrades

The meeting marks the first DAC session with the new military leadership and is expected to further accelerate India's defence modernisation and Make in India initiatives.

tweets.title: INDUS WATERS TREATY: INDIA REJECTS HAGUE RULING India has rejected the Hague-based Court of Arbitration's ruling on the Indus Waters Treaty, maintaining that the court lacks jurisdiction because the treaty mandates a sequential dispute resolution process, starting with the Permanent Indus Commission, followed by a Neutral Expert, and only then arbitration. New Delhi also reiterated that the Indus Waters Treaty remains in abeyance following the Pahalgam terrorist attack and has asserted its right to fully utilize the waters allocated to India under the treaty. India continues to advocate a comprehensive review and modification of the 1960 treaty in light of current realities.
tweets.content:
INDUS WATERS TREATY: INDIA REJECTS HAGUE RULING

India has rejected the Hague-based Court of Arbitration's ruling on the Indus Waters Treaty, maintaining that the court lacks jurisdiction because the treaty mandates a sequential dispute resolution process, starting with the Permanent Indus Commission, followed by a Neutral Expert, and only then arbitration.

New Delhi also reiterated that the Indus Waters Treaty remains in abeyance following the Pahalgam terrorist attack and has asserted its right to fully utilize the waters allocated to India under the treaty.

India continues to advocate a comprehensive review and modification of the 1960 treaty in light of current realities.

tweets.title: REPORT: Ukraine War Casualties Surpass 2 Million A new study by the Center for Strategic and International Studies estimates that total casualties in the Russia–Ukraine war have exceeded 2 million since 2022. Findings: 🇷🇺 Russia has suffered ~1.4 million casualties, including an estimated 450,000 killed. Russia is reportedly losing 30,000–34,000 troops per month, exceeding its estimated monthly recruitment of 27,000. 🇺🇦 Ukraine has recently gained more territory than it has lost, while expanding long-range strikes into Russia. Ukrainian forces have suffered between 525,000 and 625,000 total casualties, including 125,000 to 150,000 fatalities since February 2022. Ukraine maintains roughly 250,000 troops on the active front line, directly facing an estimated 400,000 Russian troops. The figures are estimates from CSIS and have not been independently verified by the governments involved.

tweets.title: Indian Army Begins Rollout of Integrated Battle Groups (IBGs) The Indian Army has begun implementing the Integrated Battle Group (IBG) concept, starting with the China-focused 17 Mountain Strike Corps. - IBGs are self-sufficient, agile combat formations of around 5,000 personnel, designed to launch offensive operations in under 24 hours. - The 23 Division will be the first to be reorganised into two IBGs, with the rollout expected to continue through mid-2027. - Each IBG will integrate infantry, armour, artillery, engineers, and signals under a single command. tweets.content:
Indian Army Begins Rollout of Integrated Battle Groups (IBGs)

The Indian Army has begun implementing the Integrated Battle Group (IBG) concept, starting with the China-focused 17 Mountain Strike Corps.

- IBGs are self-sufficient, agile combat formations of around 5,000 personnel, designed to launch offensive operations in under 24 hours.

- The 23 Division will be the first to be reorganised into two IBGs, with the rollout expected to continue through mid-2027.

- Each IBG will integrate infantry, armour, artillery, engineers, and signals under a single command.

Originally envisioned by Gen Bipin Rawat, the IBG concept is a key part of the Army's transformation towards faster, more flexible, and theatre-specific operations along India's borders.

tweets.title: UPDATE: US–Iran Talks in Doha End Without Major Breakthrough The latest round of indirect U.S.–Iran talks in Doha concluded with no major breakthrough, but both sides agreed to continue negotiations. Key discussion points included: - Freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz - Release of $6 billion in frozen Iranian funds - Establishing a direct communication channel to manage future disputes Notably, Iran's nuclear programme was not discussed, with both sides indicating it will be addressed in a future round. Qatar described the talks as making "positive progress", while U.S. President Donald Trump said discussions on limiting Iran's nuclear programme were "moving along well." The next round of talks is expected after the funeral ceremonies for former Iranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei.
tweets.content:
UPDATE: US–Iran Talks in Doha End Without Major Breakthrough

The latest round of indirect U.S.–Iran talks in Doha concluded with no major breakthrough, but both sides agreed to continue negotiations.

Key discussion points included:

- Freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz
- Release of $6 billion in frozen Iranian funds
- Establishing a direct communication channel to manage future disputes

Notably, Iran's nuclear programme was not discussed, with both sides indicating it will be addressed in a future round.

Qatar described the talks as making "positive progress", while U.S. President Donald Trump said discussions on limiting Iran's nuclear programme were "moving along well."

The next round of talks is expected after the funeral ceremonies for former Iranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei.
